section.title: Written Assignments
section.p: Please see the for the homework due dates.
As the term progresses, more written assignments will be added to the list here. These should be turned in no later than their posted due dates. They can be turn in in-person, or uploaded in D2L.

Written assignments are collectively worth 18% of your final grade. You cannot expect to do well in this course unless you are turning these assignments in. These are where you will get to develop your mathematical writing skills and work on your ability to give a convincing logical argument.

The most important thing to understand about your written homework is that you are not merely finding and giving answers. You are explaining to someone what the problem was, how you handled it, and summarizing the results. In general, your write-ups should respect these four C's:
self-Containment
Your write-up should clearly and explicitly tell the reader what you are trying to accomplish. The most straightforward way to do this is to simply copy the entire exercise statement as the first thing in your write-up.
Clarity
Am I able to read your work? Is your writing legible? Have you written complete sentences that make sense, where appropriate? If you have charts, graphs, and mathematical expressions, have you clearly indicated what they represent? Generally, any graphs need their axes labeled with regularly spaced tick marks, and they need some kind of overall title. If something about your write-up causes me to pause and wonder what you mean, you will lose clarity points.
Convincing
Even if your writing is clear, it might not be logically convincing. For example, I rode my bike to school today, so I only had $5 in my wallet. is clear in the sense that the sentence structure is fine. But I don't understand why riding your bike to school implies that you only have $5 in your wallet. The logical progression of your write-up should convince your reader that you know what you are talking about, and that each statement you write is correct based on other things you have already established in your write-up.
Conclusion
At the end of any write-up, it should be crystal clear what is your actual answer to the original question. In some cases, this will mean that you should write an actual conclusion sentence.
Each exercise will be scored out of 4 points, one for each of these categories. A zero in some category doesn't really mean 0%, it just means I felt there was significant room for improvement and you coudl try again.

paragraphs.title: Written HW 1
paragraphs.p: This assignment has six exercises from the textbook.
Section 9.1 #2. Sketch the points (0,5,2), (4,0,-1), (2,4,6), and (1,-1,2) on a single set of coordinate axes.

Notes. Look how Figure 1 in Section 9.1 is drawn. Notice how the x-axis and the y-axis are not symmetric (the x-axis is drawn in more of a downward direction than the y-axis). If you draw your axes symmetrically, it can be more difficult for your readers to understand that it is a three-dimensional image.

Notes. When drawing vectors as arrows it is not relevant where the starting position is. You do not need to draw all of the vectors with the same starting position (but you can if you want to). What matters is the direction and the length of the arrow that you draw.)
Section 9.2 #43. Use vectors to prove that the line joining the midpoints of two sides of a triangle is parallel to the third side and half its length.

Notes. Sketch a triangle, preferable one that is not a right triangle and all three sides have different lengths. Make two of the edges into arrows that emanate from one of the corners. You can label these as vectors however you like. Now pick a direction to make the third side an arrow as well. What is this vector in terms of the labels you gave to the first two vectors? Continue answering the exercise now that you have this diagram to help you think it through.

Section 9.3 #14. A street vendor sells a hamburgers, b hot dogs, and c soft drinks on a given day. They charge $2 for a hamburger, $1.50 for a hot dog, and $1 for a soft drink. If \vec{A}=\langle a,b,c\rangle and \vec{P}=\langle 2,1.5,1\rangle, what is the meaning of the dot product \vec{A}\cdot\vec{P}?
Notes. As always, make sure you are following the four C's that I use to assess written work. Your write-up to this exercise should be more than a one-line sentence or phrase.
Section 9.3 #33. Show that the vector \mathrm{orth}_{\vec{a}}\,\vec{b}=\vec{b}-\mathrm{proj}_{\vec{a}}\,\vec{b} is orthogonal to \vec{a}. (It is called an orthogonal projection of \vec{b}.

This exercise is defining what \mathrm{orth}_{\vec{a}}\,\vec{b} means in terms of \mathrm{proj}_{\vec{a}}\,\vec{b} which is defined in the section. Your job is to convince the reader that \mathrm{orth}_{\vec{a}}\,\vec{b} and \vec{a} are orthogonal.
Section 9.4 #18. Find the vector, not with determinants, but by using properties of cross products. (\hat{\imath}+\hat{\jmath})\times(\hat{\imath}-\hat{\jmath})

Notes. Do not answer this by writing \hat{\imath} as \langle1,0,0\rangle, etc. Instead, use the algebraic properties of the cross product (these are listed in a box in the section, following Example 2). At some point, you will have to think about the vectors \hat{\imath}, \hat{\jmath}, and \hat{k}; and the right-hand rule and how they interact with eachother via the cross product. How you find the final answer should be explained to your reader.

paragraphs.title: Written HW 2
paragraphs.p: This assignment has four exercises from the textbook.
Section 9.5 #34. Use intercepts to help sketch the plane. 3x+y+2z=6

Notes. This should be done by hand, not with a computer plotting it for you. Figure 7 might be a model for you to follow. However you should make it clear that you are plotting an entire plane, not just a triangle.
Section 9.5 #61. Show that the lines with symmetric equations x=y=z and x+1=y/2=z/3 are skew, and find the distance between these lines.

Notes. To show that the two lines are skew, first you could assume that they are not skew. So you could assume there are some specific numbers x_0, y_0, and z_0 that are on both of the two lines. What does the first line's symmetric equtaions tell you about x_0, y_0, and z_0? And then what does the second line's symmetric equtaions tell you about x_0, y_0, and z_0?
Section 9.6 #7. Find and sketch the domain of the function f(x,y)=\sqrt{1-x^2}-\sqrt{1-y^2}.

Notes. This should be done by hand, not with a computer plotting it for you. You are plotting a 2D image because you are plotting the domain of this function of two variables. You should explain to your reader with some words (and possibly math expressions) how you know that the domain you sketch is
Section 9.6 #12. Sketch a graph of the function f(x,y)=\cos(x).

Notes. This should be done by hand, not with a computer plotting it for you. Notice how y is not present in the function's formula. So you could plot one rib of the graph for y=0 and then know that more ribs are just translations of that first rib in the y-direction.

paragraphs.title: Written HW 3
paragraphs.p: This assignment has seven exercises from the textbook.

Section 9.7 #4. Plot the point whose cylindrical coordinates are given. Then find the rectangular coordinates of the point.
(1,\pi,e)
(1,3\pi/2,2)

Notes. This should be done by hand, not with a computer plotting it for you. Your image should make it clear to a reader how each of the three cylindrical coordinate values are present in your picture.
Section 9.7 #26. Sketch the solid described by the given inequalities. 0\leq\theta\leq\pi/2, r\leq z\leq2.

Notes. This should be done by hand, not with a computer plotting it for you. You might want to sketch the solid from more than one angle, unless you feel that your first sketch does a good job of fully communicating the shape. The first inequality here tells you something about which octants this shape can be in. With the second inequality, I suggest imagining a few fixed values for z, like z=0, z=1, and z=2. Each of these is a plane, and then you get to understand what a certain cross-section of the sold looks like.
Section 10.1 #6. Sketch the curve with the given vector equation. Indicate with an arrow the direction in which t increases. \vec{r}(t)=\langle t^2, t^3\rangle.
Notes. This should be done by hand, not with a computer plotting it for you.
Section 10.1 #8. Sketch the curve with the given vector equation. Indicate with an arrow the direction in which t increases. \vec{r}(t)=\langle \sin\pi t, t, \cos \pi t\rangle.
Notes. This should be done by hand, not with a computer plotting it for you.
Section 10.1 #25. Show that the curve with parameteric equations x=t\cos t, y=t\sin t, z=t lies on eth cone z^2=x^2+y^2, and use this fact to help sketch the curve.
Notes. This should be done by hand, not with a computer plotting it for you.

Section 10.2 #1. The figure shows a curve C given by a vector function \vec{r}(t).
Draw the vectors \vec{r}(4.5)-\vec{r}(4) and \vec{r}(4.2)-\vec{r}(4).
Draw the vectors \frac{\vec{r}(4.5)-\vec{r}(4)}{0.5} and \frac{\vec{r}(4.2)-\vec{r}(4)}{0.2}.
Write expressions for \vec{r}'(4) and the unit tangent vector \vec{T}(4).
Draw the vector \vec{T}(4).
[You will have to find the figure in the textbook.]

Notes. Copy the figure from the textbook exactly. If you prefer, you can print a picture of that figure. But then you must hand draw what is asked of you. The image might become cluttered, and it might help with your clarity if you make separate pictures for parts (a), (b), and (d). In part (c), one of the two expressions involves the limit operator, and the other can just reference the first.

li.p: Sketch the position vector \vec{r}(t) and the tangent vector \vec{r}'(t) for t=1.

paragraphs.p: Notes. This should be done by hand. In part (c), recall that you can sketch the same vector in multiple places if you like.

paragraphs.title: Written HW 4
paragraphs.p: This assignment has four exercises from the textbook.

Section 10.3 #16. Reparametrize the curve \vec{r}(t)=\left(\frac{2}{t^2+1}-1\right)\hat{\imath}+\frac{2t}{t^2+1}\hat{\jmath} with respect to arc length measured from the point (1,0) in the direction of increasing t. Express the reparametrization in the simplest form. What can you conclude about the curve?

Notes. First you will apply the procedure for parametrizing by arc length. You will need several trig identitites to simplify the reparametrized expression, and here are some that may help: \tan^2(x)+1=\sec^2(x), \frac{1}{\sec(x)}=\cos(x), \tan(x)\cos(x)=\sin(x), 2\cos^2(x/2)-1=\cos(x), 2\sin(x/2)\cos(x/2)=\sin(x).
Section 10.3 #30. At what point does the curve y=\ln x have the maximum curvature? What happens to the curvature as x\to\infty?

Notes. This curve is expressed as y=f(x), so it is convenient to find its curvature at x using Formula 11 form the section. Once you locate where the curvature is maximal, you should inlcude a plot of this function and mark where that maximal curvature happens. Visually, it should seem reasonable that the curvature is maximal there. Don't miss the last part of this exercise about x\to\infty. Your answer to that part should come from your curvature formula, but also the graph should visually validate your answer.

li.p: Is the curvature of the curve C shown in the figure greater at P or at Q? Explain.
[You will have to find the figure in the textbook.]

ol.li: Estimate the curvature at P and at Q by sketching the osculating circles at those points.

paragraphs.p: Notes. For part (b), recall that the radius of the osculating circle at a point is the reciprocal of the curvature at that point.

Section 10.3 #49. Find the equations of the osculating circles of the ellipse 9x^2+4y^2=36 at the points (2,0) and (0,3). Use a graphing calculator or computer to graph the ellipse and both osculating circles on the same screen.

Notes. If you divide each side of the ellipse equation by 36, it will be in standard form where you can identify the two radii of the ellipse. Use what you know about circular motion to parametrize the curve with a parameter t. (I do not recommend solving the ellipse equaiton for y and using Formula 11 to find curvature. In theory that would work but the formulas will be messy and you would have to take a limit as x approaches 2 from below.)
In your graph, the circles ought to look like they fit well with the ellipse. If not, it is a sign that you made an error.

paragraphs.title: Written HW 5
paragraphs.p: This assignment has six exercises from the textbook.
Section 10.4 #2. The figure shows the path of a particle that moves with position vector \vec{r}(t) at time t.
[You will have to find the figure in the textbook.]

li.p: Draw a vector that represents the average velocity of the particle over the time interval 2\leq t\leq2.4.
Draw a vector that represents the average velocity of the particle over the time interval 1.5\leq t\leq2.
Write an expression for the velocity vector \vec{v}(2).
Draw an approxiamtion to the vector \vec{v}(2) and estimate the speed of the particle at t=2.

paragraphs.p: Notes. When you draw the vectors that are asked for in (a) and (b), draw them with their tails at the appropriate place: \vec{r}(2). Remember that when 0.4 units of time pass, and you divide by 0.4, that is the same as multiplying by 2.5. Take note that the two axes have a scale, so it is possible for you to estimate how long things are that you draw.

Section 10.4 #26. A batter hits a baseball 3 ft above the ground toward the center field fence, which is 10 ft high and 400 ft from home plate. The ball leaves the bat with speed 115 ft/s at an angle of 50^{\circ} above the horizontal. Is it a home run? (In other words, does the ball clear the fence?)
Notes. You will need to know the acceleration due to gravity on Earth in feet per second. You can treat this as a 2D vector problem. I recommend drawing some pictures.
Section 11.1 #5. Find and sketch the domain of the function f(x,y)=\ln(9-x^2-9y^2). What is the range of f?
Section 11.1 #10. Shown is a contour map of atmospheric pressure in North America on August 12, 2008. On the level curves (called isobars) the pressure is indicated in millibars (mb).
[You will have to find the figure in the textbook.]

li.p: Estimate the pressure at C (Chicago), N (Nashville), S (San Francisco), and V (Vancouver).
At which of these locations were the winds strongest?

paragraphs.p: Notes. Remember that a good introduction (which is one point for the exercise) includes any graphs or images that help the reader understand what your task is. For part (b), make sure that you explain why you have chosen a particular city.
Section 11.1 #27. Sketch both a contour map and a graph (3D) of the function and compare them. f(x,y)=x^2+9y^2

Notes. It may help to recall that if you have an equation of the form \frac{x^2}{a^2}+\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1, it makes an ellipse centered at the origin, with x-radius a and y-radius b. If you set x^2+9y^2 equal to some number, you can divide by that number and then also use a little algebra rearrangement to get that form of an ellipse equation.

Notes. Try approaching (0,0) using curves of the form y=p(x) or y=q(x). You are trying to find two paths that reveal different limit behavior, so you can conclude that this limit overall does not exist.

paragraphs.title: Written HW 6
paragraphs.p: This assignment has five exercises from the textbook.

Section 11.3 #4. The wave heights h in the open sea depend on the speed v of the wind and the length of time t that the wind has been blowing at that speed. Values of the function h=f(v,t) are recorded in feet in the following table.
[You will have to find the table in the textbook.]

li.p: What are the meanings of the partial derivatives \frac{\partial h}{\partial v} and \frac{\partial h}{\partial t}?
Estimate the values of f_v(40,15) and f_t(40,15). What are the practical interpretations of these values?
What appears to be the value of the following limit? \lim_{t\to\infty}\frac{\partial h}{\partial t}

paragraphs.p: Notes. For part (a), think about the units that would be attached to each partial derivative.

Section 11.3 #58. Determine the signs of the partial derivatives for the function f whose graph is shown.
f_x(1,2)
f_y(1,2)
f_x(-1,2)
f_y(-1,2)
f_{xx}(-1,2)
f_{yy}(-1,2)
f_{xy}(1,2)
f_{xy}(-1,2)
Notes. For each part, you should write roughly one sentence that explains why you are deciding the way you are deciding. A complete introduction would include a copy of the given graph.
Section 11.3 #9. The following surfaces, labeled a, b, and c, are graphs of a function f and its partial derivatives f_x and f_y. Identify each surface and give reasons for your choices.
[You will have to find the graphs in the textbook.]
Notes. A complete introduction would include copies of the given graphs. Make sure that you try to explain to your reader why you are making the choices you are making.
Notes. This problem is all about finding partial derivatives in a real context, and interpreting them.

Section 11.4 #8. Graph the surface and the tangent plane at the given point. (Choose the domain and viewpoint so that you get a good view of both the surface and the tangent plane.) Then zoom in until the surface and the tangent plane become indistinguishable.
z=\arctan(xy^2), \qquad (1,1,\pi/4)

Notes. I am expecting you to show the steps/work for finding the tangent plane's equation. Then use a 3D graphing app (such as GeoGebra's 3D calculator) to make two images. One should be from a distance, the other zoomed in as requested. Include the computer-generated images in your write-up.

paragraphs.title: Written HW 7
paragraphs.p: This assignment has six exercises from the textbook.

Section 11.5 #34. Wheat production W in a given year depends on the average temperature T and the annual rainfall R. Scientists estimate that the average temperature is rising at a rate of 0.15^{\circ}\mathrm{C}/\mathrm{year} and rainfall is decreasing at a rate of 0.1\,\mathrm{cm}/\mathrm{year}. They also estimate that, at current prduction levels, \partial W/\partial T=-2 and \partial W/\partial R=8.
What is the significance of the signs of these partial derivatives?
Estimate the current rate of change of wheat production, dW/dt.
Notes. For part (a), you are trying to say that as something increases, something else increases/decreases. Just be specific about what is increasing, etc.

Section 11.5 #42. If a sound with frequency f_s is produced by a source traveling along a line with speed v_s and an observer is traveling with speed v_o along the same line from the opposite direction toward the source, then the frequency of the sound heard by the observer is f_o=\left(\frac{c+v_o}{c-v_s}\right)f_s where c is the speed of sound, about 332 m/s. (This is the Doppler effect.) Suppose that, at a particular moment, you are in a train traveling at 34\,\mathrm{m}/\mathrm{s} and accelerating at 1.2\,\mathrm{m}/\mathrm{s}^2. A train is approaching you from the opposite direction on the other track at 40\,\mathrm{m}/\mathrm{s}, accelerating at 1.4\,\mathrm{m}/\mathrm{s}^2, and sounds its whistle, which has a frequency of 460\,\mathrm{Hz}. At that instant, what is the perceived frequency that you hear and how fast is it changing?

Notes. You are the observer. This question asks you to find two things. One is just f_o at that moment, and you simply use the Doppler effect equatin to get that. But this also asks for \frac{df_o}{dt} at that moment, and you will use the Chain Rule to find it. Note that c and f_s are constants. But v_o and v_s are not constant.

Section 11.6 #32. Suppose you are climbing a hill whose shape is given by the equation z=1000-0.005x^2-0.01y^2, where x, y, and z are measured in meters, and you are standing on a point with coordinates (60,40,966). The positive x-axis points east, and the positive y-axis points north.
If you walk due south, will you start to ascend or descend? At what rate?
If you walk northwest, will you start to ascend or descend? At what rate?
In which direction is the slope largest? What is the rate of ascent in that direction? At what angle above the horizontal does the path in that direction begin?

Notes. Parts (a) and (b) are only asking for a certin directional derivative. Part (c) is asking for serveral things. You will need to understand several things about the gradient vector at that location to answer all of the questions in part (c).

Section 11.7 #33. For functions of one variable it is impossible for a continuous function to have two local maxima and no local minimum. But for functions of two variables such functions exist. Show that the function f(x,y)=-(x^2-1)^2-(x^2y-x-1)^2 has only two critical points, but has local maxima at both of them. Then use a computer to produce a graph with carefully chose domain and viewpoint to see how this is possible.

Notes. Is it clear why this is impossible for a continuous function of one variable? In between the two maxima, you would have to have a minimum. Anyway, show what is asked for about a two-variable function. Also, be sure to include a copy of the graph that you make using GeoGebra, Desmos, etc.

Section 11.7 #46. The base of an aquarium with given volume V is made of slate and the sides are made of glass. If slate costs five times as much (per unit area) as glass, find the dimensions of the aquarium that minimize the cost of materials.

Notes. At first you have three variables: x, y, z. But since the volume is fixed at V, you can replace z with \frac{V}{xy} and only have two variables. Now do something simple like assume the glass costs one dollar per unit of area, and calclulate the total glass cost in terms of x and y. Then do the same for the slate, but assuming it costs five dollars per unit area. (If the costs are different, it won't matter as long as the ratio of the per-unit-area costs is still five.) Add it all up to get the total cost, and this is the function to optimize. In the end, report the three dimensions of the aquarium.
You could tackle this exercise using Lagrange multipliers from section 11.8. But don't do that. The purpose here is to demonstrate the approach outlined above.
Section 11.8 #38. Use Lagrange multipliers to give an alternate solution to Exercise #46 from Section 11.7.

OK, now you have a function of three variables x, y, z. Rather than eliminate z, use the constraint V=xyz, and work with a simpler looking cost function that is in terms of x, y, and z. Make sure you get the same result as with the previous exercise.

paragraphs.title: Written HW 8
paragraphs.p: This assignment has five exercises from the textbook.
Section 12.2 #32. Graph the solid that lies between the surface z=2xy/\left(x^2+1\right) and the plane z=x+2y and is bounded by the planes x=0, x=2, y=0, and y=4. Then find its volume.

Notes. You should use a 3D graphing app to plot the six surfaces that are described here. Typically, 3D graphing apps will use trasparency to allow you to see surfaces through the other side of surfaces, and you will want that to make this solid visible. Capture at least two images from different viewpoints so your reader can really see what this looks like. Then to find the volume, set up the appropriate integral and find it by hand.
Section 12.3 #38. Sketch the solid whose volume is given by the iterated integral \int_0^1\int_0^{1-x^2}(1-x)\,dy\,dx

Notes. This is only asking you to amke the sketch, not also find the volume (although that would be easy to do). You should attempt to sketch the solid by hand. First make your axis system in the usual way with the x-axis pointing a bit down and to the left, the y-axis pointing mostly to the right, and the z-axis pointing up. The bounds on the iterated integral define curves/lines you should attempt to sketch in the xy-plane. The function being integrated is a z-value, and that defines for you a top to this object.

Section 12.3 #60. In evaluating a double integral over a region D, a sum of iterated integral was obtained as follows: \iint_D f(x,y)\,dA=\int_0^1\int_0^{2y}f(x,y)\,dx\,dy+\int_1^3\int_0^{3-y}f(x,y)\,dx\,dy Sketch the region D and express the double integral as an iterated integral with reversed order of integration.

Notes. The sketch you make for this exercise is a 2D sketch of D, not a 3D sketch. The poit of the exercise is that if you can use the sum of two iterated integrals to understand what the region D actually looks like, then if you change the order of integration, it is actually just a single iterated integral (not a sum of two iterated integrals).
Section 12.4 #5. Sketch the region whose area is given by the integral and evaluate the integral: \int_{\pi}^{2\pi}\int_4^7r\,dr\,d\theta

Notes. The first step is to sketch the shape. Since the integrand is r\,dr\,d\theta, which is polar coordinates is dA, the limits on the two integrals define for you the shape. After you understand the shape, you are welcome to either actually do the integral using antiderivatives, or use basic geometry area formulas.

Section 12.4 #31. A swimming pool is circular with a 40-ft diameter. The depth is constant along east-west lines and increases linearly from 2 ft at the south end to 7 ft at the north end. Find the volume of water in the pool.

Notes. You might benefit from sketching this 3D object, as well as sketching the 2D region over which you will integrate. You need to integrate the depth (in feet) times dA, which is in square feet. Remember to use the polar coordinates expression for dA. The depth of the pool is easiest to express as a function of y (given the orientation with the shallow end at the southern point and deep end at the northern point) but you will want to convert that into polar coordinates.
